label: fiction
title: The Borrowed Voice
setting: A post-production studio, a cloned manager's voice, and a late-night audio export in 2026.
charge: Using synthetic audio to extort money from a colleague
elements: Evan made or participated in making and sending the synthetic audio to Nima.
The message demanded money and threatened harm to Nima's employment if the demand was refused or reported.
Evan acted deliberately and dishonestly, intending to obtain the money.
hook: The voice on the call belongs to a manager. The fear in it belongs to a colleague. The breaths belong to someone else.

accused.human: Evan Rusk records local theatre and has spent years teaching free sound workshops to young performers.
accused.if_guilty: A conviction means prison, loss of a creative career, and permanent suspicion around every recording he has made.

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: The manager's voice was synthetic, but the prosecution does not ask you to identify its maker by sound alone. Beneath that voice were Evan's breath track and whispered count. On Evan's laptop, temporary files preserve those layers in order, and his hardware key authenticated the late-night export. The prosecution says that connected record proves a deliberate extortion.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: Public interviews supplied the manager's voice, while students and contractors used the same microphones, laptop, and sample drives during Evan's workshops. A hardware-key record identifies a device, not the hand holding it. The defence says the studio's loose access allowed someone else to assemble familiar material and leave Evan carrying the suspicion.

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: Anyone could copy the manager's public voice. The harder question is who arranged the hidden layers and exported them. Temporary files on Evan's laptop contain his breath track, the cloned speech, and the masking tone in sequence. His hardware key opened that session and exported it while his badge remained inside. The prosecution says those independent records prove authorship and intent.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: The records prove that Evan's equipment and key were used, not that Evan used them. Contractors had copied workshop files, temporary badges remained active, and the console bay had no camera. Evan does not have to identify another user or explain how every file arrived. If the prosecution has not excluded that practical access, identity remains unproved and your verdict must be not guilty.

epilogue: Nima received counselling and returned on a different production team. Evan's workshops stopped during the investigation, and several young performers lost their free recording sessions. The studio later required personal key PINs, added a camera to the console bay, and logged every copied voice sample. It also removed Nima's review notes from shared handover files.

beats.speaker: w1
beats.text: The message used my manager's voice and repeated a mistake from my performance review. It demanded twelve thousand dollars within two days. If I reported it, the voice said my job would disappear and the review would be sent to future employers. I saved the audio and payment instructions before speaking to anyone at the studio.
beats.reveal_note: The first impression came from the synthetic recording that resembled the manager; its value depended on whether the later evidence could overcome this limit: a convincing cloned voice identifies a source library, not necessarily its maker.

beats.speaker: w2
beats.text: I examined the recording and the studio devices. The manager's speech was generated from public interviews, so that voice does not identify its maker. Beneath it were four breaths and a whispered count. Their microphone signature and room reflection matched an unedited tutorial recorded on Evan's laptop earlier that evening.
beats.reveal_note: This witness fixed the practical setting before suspicion centred on Evan Rusk, giving the room context for the records that followed.

beats.speaker: dc
beats.text: Exhibit three is the voice-source report. Every phrase spoken in the manager's cloned voice came from six public interviews. The software used to assemble it is freely available, and no hidden marker identifies a particular user. The report therefore links the recording to a public source library, not to Evan or any other individual.
beats.reveal_note: The challenged record mattered because it exposed the gap between appearance and proof: a convincing cloned voice identifies a source library, not necessarily its maker.

beats.speaker: w1
beats.text: The manager discussed that review mistake at a team meeting? Only the mistake, not the written rating. Five production staff were present. The manager's interviews were public, so neither the voice nor the mistake was known only to Evan? Correct. The interviews were on the company channel, and several people knew about the mistake.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: The manager discussed that review mistake at a team meeting?

turns.speaker: w1
turns.text: Only the mistake, not the written rating. Five production staff were present.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: The manager's interviews were public, so neither the voice nor the mistake was known only to Evan?

turns.speaker: w1
turns.text: Correct. The interviews were on the company channel, and several people knew about the mistake.

beats.reveal_note: Cross-examination narrowed the early clue without erasing it, forcing the room to decide how much independent support remained.

beats.speaker: pc
beats.text: Exhibit five is the recovered render history from Evan's laptop. Although the project folder was deleted, temporary files preserve its layer order: cloned speech, the matching breath-and-count track, then a tone masking each edit. Evan's physical key authenticated the final export. It matches Nima's file exactly, including a compression error absent from every earlier workshop recording.
beats.reveal_note: The strongest prosecution point was this: the hidden breaths and count matched Evan’s microphone and room, while his hardware key unlocked and exported the final audio.

beats.speaker: w3
beats.text: I did not make or send that message. My laptop and microphone were used in free workshops by students and contractors, and finished tutorials sat on open sample drives. During supervised sessions, my hardware key sometimes remained in the console. A matching room, device, or tutorial proves where material came from; it does not prove who assembled it.
beats.reveal_note: The competing account was concrete rather than speculative: public samples, shared equipment, and open studio drives gave contractors access to the voices and room tones needed for a frame-up.

beats.speaker: w4
beats.text: I manage the studio's computers and access system. Three contractor badges remained active until midnight, and the console bay had no camera. Sample drives containing voices, room tones, and tutorials sat in open bays. Hardware keys did not require a PIN. Our log records a key's certificate, but it cannot show which authorised person was holding that key.
beats.reveal_note: This testimony tested whether the system record described a person’s conduct or only an event produced around them.

beats.speaker: w3
beats.text: The recovered project was on your laptop, with breaths and a whispered count from your tutorial? Yes. That is what the examination found. Your key opened, exported, and then deleted that project while your badge was still inside. Can you identify anyone else using the laptop then? No. I was packing equipment in another room and saw nobody at the console.

turns.speaker: pc
turns.text: The recovered project was on your laptop, with breaths and a whispered count from your tutorial?

turns.speaker: w3
turns.text: Yes. That is what the examination found.

turns.speaker: pc
turns.text: Your key opened, exported, and then deleted that project while your badge was still inside. Can you identify anyone else using the laptop then?

turns.speaker: w3
turns.text: No. I was packing equipment in another room and saw nobody at the console.

beats.reveal_note: The second cross put the decisive inference under pressure and showed exactly which part of the account could still be disputed.

beats.speaker: w4
beats.text: Only Evan's key authenticated the export while his badge was still inside? Yes. Three contractor badges also remained active, the console bay had no camera, and someone could copy workshop files without another access record. So the key log cannot tell this jury who held the key? Correct to each point. It records no face, fingerprint, or PIN.

turns.speaker: pc
turns.text: Only Evan's key authenticated the export while his badge was still inside?

turns.speaker: pc
turns.text: Three contractor badges also remained active, the console bay had no camera, and someone could copy workshop files without another access record. So the key log cannot tell this jury who held the key?

turns.speaker: w4
turns.text: Correct to each point. It records no face, fingerprint, or PIN.

beats.reveal_note: The final witness supplied a separate check on the timeline, reducing the risk that one attractive clue would decide the case alone.

beats.speaker: judge
beats.text: Do not research voice cloning, the studio, or anyone involved. The prosecution must make you sure Evan participated in making and sending the message, knowingly demanded money through a threat to Nima's employment, and intended to obtain that money dishonestly. A device record is not automatically a person. Evan does not have to identify another user or explain every suspicious file.
beats.reveal_note: The direction kept the burden where it belonged: a convincing cloned voice identifies a source library, not necessarily its maker, and no gap in the defence could substitute for proof of every element.

twist: The manager's cloned voice was the loudest clue and the least identifying: every phrase came from public interviews. The more specific trail was hidden underneath. Evan's breath-and-count track sat inside a recovered project on his laptop, followed by the final export and deletion authenticated with his physical key while his badge remained inside.

jurors.label: Juror 2
jurors.persona: A cautious holdout who wants the decisive record stated without exaggeration. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 2: The open drives, active contractor badges, and camera gap make another user more than an abstract possibility.
Juror 2: A key certificate identifies a device. It does not resolve who was holding it.
pushback: Juror 2: The cloned voice sounds personal, but public interviews and a shared review story explain its raw material.
Juror 2: I want us to separate proof about Evan's equipment from proof about Evan himself.
concede: Juror 2: Evan's key, laptop, hidden breath track, and badge timing do form a specific chain.
final: Juror 2: My vote turns on whether the access gap remains reasonable after the laptop and key records are combined.

jurors.label: Juror 3
jurors.persona: An empathetic juror who notices harm but refuses to let sympathy replace proof. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 3: The recovered project matches Nima's file exactly, down to a compression error absent from earlier tutorials.
Juror 3: Evan's key authenticated the export while his badge remained inside. Those records support each other.
pushback: Juror 3: The cloned manager's voice adds emotion, not identity. The hidden layers and export are the useful evidence.
Juror 3: Another person could hold Evan's key, but that person also needed his laptop and workshop material.
concede: Juror 3: I accept that the console bay had weak security and no camera.
final: Juror 3: I am deciding whether shared access reasonably explains every Evan-specific record arriving in the same project.

jurors.label: Juror 4
jurors.persona: A systems-minded juror alert to both automation errors and deliberate manipulation. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 4: Evan need not identify the other user. The prosecution must prove he made and sent the threat.
pushback: Juror 4: The log has no face, fingerprint, or PIN. We cannot silently add a person to it.
Juror 4: Public voice samples are not identity evidence, however convincing the finished recording sounds.
concede: Juror 4: The exact project match and Evan's badge timing make the key record harder to dismiss.
holdout: Juror 4: I am holding until we test whether an active contractor could realistically reach the laptop and key.
Juror 4: Evan's silence about another user cannot repair any gap in the prosecution case.
final: Juror 4: Identity must be proved from the records, not from Evan's inability to name someone else.

jurors.label: Juror 5
jurors.persona: A quiet questioner who tests what the strongest theory still leaves unexplained. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 5: Layering the breath track, cloned speech, and masking tone shows deliberate construction, not an accidental export.
pushback: Juror 5: The method is clear. The harder question is whether Evan or another authorised person carried it out.
Juror 5: I give the public voice no identifying weight; the temporary files deserve much more.
concede: Juror 5: Weak studio security is real, even though it does not explain the full project history.
burden_correct: Juror 5: Evan does not have to prove who borrowed the key. The prosecution must prove it was him.
Juror 5: An unexplained file may be evidence, but it does not move the burden onto Evan.
final: Juror 5: I am applying the burden separately to authorship, the threat, and the dishonest demand.

jurors.label: Juror 6
jurors.persona: A steady foreperson who makes the room distinguish suspicion from proof. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 6: The project history shows the threatening file being assembled and exported in one controlled sequence.
Juror 6: The matching compression error connects Evan's recovered render to the file Nima actually received.
pushback: Juror 6: Let's place the badge, key, render, deletion, and delivery in order before arguing about identity.
Juror 6: The room should acknowledge the open console without letting that one weakness erase every other record.
concede: Juror 6: I agree that the manager's synthetic voice could have been created by many people.
final: Juror 6: Our verdict should follow the connected timeline, with no single dramatic clue carrying the case.

jurors.label: Juror 7
jurors.persona: A careful note-taker who separates a striking clue from the chain needed to prove identity. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 7: The temporary files and exact compression error independently connect Evan's laptop to Nima's message.
Juror 7: The key export and badge timing make the digital sequence more persuasive than the cloned voice.
pushback: Juror 7: The key log still identifies an object, and the unobserved console leaves room for another user.
Juror 7: I am moving between the project history and the studio's loose access because both are concrete.
concede: Juror 7: I accept that public interviews explain the voice without identifying its maker.
final: Juror 7: The answer depends on whether the digital sequence overcomes the real access gap.

jurors.label: Juror 8
jurors.persona: A practical juror who tests digital records against ordinary workplace habits. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 8: Evan's key, laptop, badge, and tutorial layer now look like one connected course of conduct.
Juror 8: The exact file error persuaded me because it links the recovered project to the delivered threat.
pushback: Juror 8: Shared workshop access weakens the identity claim, especially because the console had no camera.
Juror 8: If the key can be borrowed without a PIN, the certificate is not the same as a person.
concede: Juror 8: I changed my view because the project match explains more than the cloned voice alone.
final: Juror 8: I can explain my vote from the records without assuming the synthetic voice identifies anyone.

jurors.label: Juror 9
jurors.persona: A sceptic who distrusts polished system evidence until its collection method is clear. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 9: Evan's account leaves the overnight export and deletion unexplained, which weakens his denial.
Juror 9: His workshop explanation covers source material, but not the exact threatening project recovered from his laptop.
pushback: Juror 9: Evan sounded direct, but I must compare his denial with the access records and recovered files.
Juror 9: Credibility helps me weigh his evidence; it cannot substitute for testing the digital record.
concede: Juror 9: A sincere witness can still be mistaken about who used equipment left in a shared studio.
final: Juror 9: I am weighing Evan's denial against the records without treating either as automatically decisive.

jurors.label: Juror 10
jurors.persona: A rule-focused juror who brings the room back to the prosecution burden. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 10: Evan's key and badge appear alongside a project unique to the delivered threat. That strongly supports identity.
pushback: Juror 10: The public voice and shared review story add context, but neither identifies the maker.
burden_drift: Juror 10: If Evan says another person used his key and laptop, I keep wanting him to say who.
Juror 10: Evan's inability to explain the deleted project troubles me, even though I know the Crown carries the burden.
concede: Juror 10: The lack of a camera leaves an access gap that the key certificate cannot close by itself.
burden_correct: Juror 10: Evan does not have to name another user. The prosecution must prove he made and sent the threat.
final: Juror 10: My vote must rest on the prosecution's proof, not on answers Evan could not provide.

jurors.label: Juror 11
jurors.persona: A patient listener who changes position when a timeline finally accounts for every event. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 11: The matching project, export key, and badge timing create a timeline I can test rather than guess.
Juror 11: The digital trail becomes persuasive when each independent record points to the same late-night session.
pushback: Juror 11: Shared files explain the ingredients, but I am still asking who assembled and exported this exact project.
Juror 11: Active contractor badges make another user possible; the full timeline decides whether that possibility is reasonable.
concede: Juror 11: I accept that the cloned voice itself proves nothing about authorship.
final: Juror 11: I followed the sequence from source material to project, export, deletion, and delivery.

jurors.label: Juror 12
jurors.persona: A blunt juror who challenges convenient memory from witnesses on both sides. In The Borrowed Voice, this juror keeps returning to the synthetic recording that resembled the manager.

agree: Juror 12: The recovered project shows a deliberate method, but the Crown still must prove Evan was its author.
Juror 12: The key and badge support identity; the public voice does not. We should keep those propositions separate.
pushback: Juror 12: A realistic synthetic voice is impressive technology, not proof of who operated it.
Juror 12: Put the key certificate beside the camera gap before deciding what either one proves.
concede: Juror 12: The compression error makes the recovered render much more specific than a shared tutorial file.
final: Juror 12: My vote follows the records that identify conduct, with the burden staying on the prosecution.
